news_summary: Exciting developments include the London Marathon expanding to a two-day event in 2027, new historical insights revealing ancient summer solstice celebrations predating Stonehenge, and promising progress on the “One Europe, One Market" initiative.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les développements marquants, on peut citer l'extension du marathon de Londres à un événement de deux jours en 2027, de nouvelles découvertes historiques révélant l'existence d'anciennes célébrations du solstice d'été antérieures à Stonehenge, ainsi que des avancées prometteuses concernant l'initiative « Une Europe, un marché ».
news_summary_es: Entre las novedades más destacadas se encuentran la ampliación del Maratón de Londres a un evento de dos días en 2027, nuevos hallazgos históricos que revelan antiguas celebraciones del solsticio de verano anteriores a Stonehenge y avances prometedores en la iniciativa «Una Europa, un mercado».

articles.title: London Marathon to be two-day event in 2027
articles.summary: The London Marathon will be held across two days in a special one-off edition of the event in 2027, organisers have confirmed.
articles.content:
The London Marathon will be held across two days in a special one-off edition of the event in 2027.

Organisers confirmed earlier this year that they were exploring ways to enable more people to take part, after a record 1.33 million people entered the ballot for next year's event.

The two events - on Saturday, 24 and Sunday, 25 April - will allow 100,000 people to take part in the London Marathon in 2027, effectively doubling the chances of success for those who entered the ballot.

The move is also expected to raise more than £150m for charities and provide a £400m boost to the UK economy, according to Hugh Brasher, chief executive of London Marathon Events (LME).

Everyone who applied to take part next year will be entered into a ballot for both the Saturday and Sunday, with the ballot results announced in early July.

"The 2027 London Marathon double is our most ambitious evolution to date - a once-in-a-generation one-time-only reimagining of what a marathon and city-wide celebration of activity can be," said Brasher.

"By expanding to 100,000 runners across two days, we're opening the door for more people, more charities and more communities to take part in the world's greatest marathon. We believe that more than £150m can be raised for good causes and the UK economy will have a £400m social and economic benefit.

"We are grateful for the vision and support of the Mayor of London, the councils on the route, the emergency services, and all the stakeholders involved who have given their support to enable this world first event to be held for London and the UK."

Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, said: "London is the sporting capital of the world and I am delighted that, for one year only in 2027, the world famous London Marathon will expand into a two-day event."

A total of 1,338,544 people applied for the 2027 London Marathon, breaking the record of 1,133,813 applicants for the 2026 event.

The London Marathon had a record 59,830 finishers in 2026 and was the biggest annual one-day fundraising event in the world, with more than £90m raised to date for charity by 2026 participants.

History was also made on the course at this year's event, as Kenya's Sabastian Sawe became the first athlete to run a sub-two-hour marathon in a competitive race.

Brasher has remained adamant that the expanded two-day event will be for one year only, following what he described as an "astonishing total of applicants" hoping to take part in 2027.

He told BBC Sport the two-day event has been nine years in the making, but after the initial idea was formed in 2017, with the intention to hold it in 2020, they failed to gain permission.

The plan is for the elite women, elite female wheelchair athletes, championship and 'good for age' women to lead the mass event one day, with the elite men, elite male wheelchair athletes, championship and 'good for age' men leading the mass event on the other.

However, London Marathon organisers said all format details will be confirmed in the coming months.

All 100,000 participants across the two days will run on the normal London Marathon route from Greenwich to Westminster.

Brasher clarified to the BBC that a ballot draw will first be held for the Sunday - the day on which the marathon is normally held - before a second draw takes place for the Saturday.

The Mini London Marathon will be held on the Friday, with more than 20,000 young participants.

Organisers say all additional income generated by the two-day format will be distributed by the London Marathon Foundation to projects that inspire activity for children and young people across London and the UK.
articles.reasoning: The London Marathon expanding to a two-day event in 2027 will allow 100,000 participants, doubling access and significantly increasing charitable fundraising (expected £150m+) and economic benefit (£400m) to the UK. This large-scale, one-time event promotes community participation, health, and charity support with broad societal impact and detailed planning.
articles.personality_title: London Marathon to double runners with special two-day event in 2027
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The London Marathon is one of the world’s biggest running events, attracting tens of thousands of runners every year. In recent years, more people than ever have wanted to join, making it hard to fit everyone who applies.

**What happened** – Organisers announced that the 2027 London Marathon will be held over two days, April 24 and 25, instead of the usual one day. This special, one-time change will allow 100,000 runners to take part, roughly twice the usual number. Everyone who applied for 2027 will be entered into a lottery for spots on both days.

**Impact** – This change means more people can join the race, doubling the chance to be selected. It is expected to raise over £150 million for charities and bring a £400 million boost to the UK economy. The event will also continue to inspire communities and encourage physical activity. The two-day marathon is a unique event planned with support from city officials and emergency services.

**What's next step** – The organisers will announce the lottery results in July 2026. They will also share more details about how runners will be split between the days. The Mini London Marathon for young runners will happen the day before, on April 23. All extra money earned will support projects that help children and young people stay active.

**One-sentence takeaway** – In 2027, the London Marathon will expand to two days, doubling participants and fundraising to make a bigger impact on charity and the community.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le marathon de Londres doublera le nombre de coureurs avec un événement spécial sur deux jours en 2027
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le marathon de Londres est l’un des plus grands événements de course au monde, attirant chaque année des dizaines de milliers de coureurs. Ces dernières années, de plus en plus de personnes ont voulu participer, ce qui rend difficile d’accueillir tous les candidats.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les organisateurs ont annoncé que le marathon de Londres 2027 se déroulera sur deux jours, les 24 et 25 avril, au lieu d’un seul jour habituel. Ce changement spécial et unique permettra à 100 000 coureurs de participer, soit environ le double du nombre habituel. Tous ceux qui ont postulé pour 2027 seront inscrits à une loterie pour les deux jours.

**Impact** – Ce changement signifie que plus de personnes pourront courir, doublant les chances d’être sélectionné. Il devrait permettre de collecter plus de 150 millions de livres pour des œuvres caritatives et d’apporter un bénéfice de 400 millions de livres à l’économie britannique. L’événement continuera aussi d’inspirer les communautés et d’encourager l’activité physique. Ce marathon sur deux jours est un événement unique organisé avec le soutien des autorités locales et des services d’urgence.

**Prochaine étape** – Les organisateurs annonceront les résultats de la loterie en juillet 2026. Ils donneront aussi plus de détails sur la répartition des coureurs entre les deux jours. Le Mini Marathon de Londres pour les jeunes aura lieu la veille, le 23 avril. Tous les revenus supplémentaires seront utilisés pour soutenir des projets qui encouragent les enfants et les jeunes à rester actifs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En 2027, le marathon de Londres s’étendra sur deux jours, doublant le nombre de participants et les fonds récoltés pour avoir un plus grand impact sur les œuvres caritatives et la communauté.
articles.personality_title_es: El maratón de Londres duplicará corredores con evento especial de dos días en 2027
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El maratón de Londres es uno de los eventos de carrera más grandes del mundo, con decenas de miles de corredores cada año. En los últimos años, muchas más personas han querido participar, lo que dificulta incluir a todos los solicitantes.

**Qué pasó** – Los organizadores anunciaron que el maratón de Londres 2027 se realizará durante dos días, el 24 y 25 de abril, en lugar de un día como siempre. Este cambio especial y único permitirá que 100,000 corredores participen, aproximadamente el doble del número habitual. Todos los que se inscribieron para 2027 entrarán en un sorteo para ambos días.

**Impacto** – Este cambio significa que más personas podrán correr, duplicando las oportunidades de ser seleccionados. Se espera recaudar más de £150 millones para organizaciones benéficas y aportar un beneficio de £400 millones a la economía del Reino Unido. El evento seguirá inspirando a las comunidades y fomentando la actividad física. Este maratón de dos días es un evento único planeado con el apoyo de las autoridades locales y servicios de emergencia.

**Próximo paso** – Los organizadores anunciarán los resultados del sorteo en julio de 2026. También compartirán más detalles sobre cómo se dividirán los corredores entre los días. El Mini Maratón de Londres para jóvenes se celebrará el día antes, el 23 de abril. Todo el dinero extra recaudado apoyará proyectos que ayuden a niños y jóvenes a mantenerse activos.

**Resumen en una frase** – En 2027, el maratón de Londres se ampliará a dos días, duplicando participantes y recaudación para tener un mayor impacto en la caridad y la comunidad.
articles.image_prompt: Two flowing ribbons, one golden and one silver, intertwine over a softly painted map of London’s iconic marathon route from Greenwich to Westminster, with stylized silhouettes of runners in motion along the path, set against a warm, pastel sky that suggests celebration and unity across two connected days.

articles.title: Ancient monument marked summer solstice centuries before Stonehenge
articles.summary: Archaeologists have discovered traces of a wooden structure built 5000 years ago, 5 kilometres from Stonehenge, which appears to have been an even older monument for marking the summer solstice
articles.content:
Archaeologists have discovered traces of a wooden structure built 5000 years ago, 5 kilometres from Stonehenge, which appears to have been an even older monument for marking the summer solstice

A reconstruction of the summer solstice celebrations as they might have appeared at Bulford thousands of years ago Marijane Porter, Wessex Archaeology

Stone Age peoples in Britain built a wooden monument to mark the summer solstice, 500 years before they began building the stone circle at Stonehenge.

Stonehenge is also aligned to the summer solstice, and the wooden monument may have been an early prototype of this. It is one of the earliest examples of a monument aligned to an astronomical phenomenon in the British Isles.

“What we have now, for the first time, is actual proof that these people were capable of capturing the movement of the sun,” said Phil Harding at Wessex Archaeology, who led the excavations, at a press conference announcing the discovery.

Stonehenge is a monument built during the Neolithic, the very end of the Stone Age. Situated on Salisbury Plain in the UK, it consists of an outer ring of vertical sarsen stones topped by horizontal lintels, a smaller inner ring of vertical bluestones and several other stones. These are surrounded by an earth bank and a ditch. This is the oldest part, built around 3100 BC, with the stones being placed over the centuries up to 1600 BC.

Some of the standing stones seem to have been carefully aligned to point to the spot on the horizon where the sun rises on the summer solstice, and to the opposing spot where the sun sets on the winter solstice. The relevant stones were erected about 2500 BC.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

Harding and his colleagues have found the remains of a nearby monument that is 500 years older. About 5 kilometres north-east of Stonehenge, there is a village called Bulford, where the UK’s Ministry of Defence wanted to house about 5000 army personnel. Before construction began, Wessex Archaeology excavated Bulford from 2015 to 2017.

The team found a cluster of pits containing grooved ware pottery, which was made by late Neolithic peoples. Radiocarbon dating suggested the pottery was from about 2950 BC. The researchers obtained 40 dates, all tightly clustered in time. “This site was being occupied for a relatively short period of time,” said Harding. “It could be something like a decade.”

“It’s a really important Middle Neolithic settlement,” says Susan Greaney at the University of Exeter, UK, who wasn’t involved in the study.

A piece of pottery found at Bulford, UK Wessex Archaeology

Two of the Bulford pits were a different shape to the others. Instead of having vertical sides, they tapered towards the bottom, going from about 1.2 metres across to just 0.5 metres. They also didn’t contain pottery, but were filled with chalk rubble. Harding and his colleagues concluded that they were postholes: they had once housed timbers a few metres tall, which were held upright by the rubble. In line with this, one of the postholes contained charcoal from an ash tree.

The two postholes are about 120 metres apart. Harding realised that a line drawn through them would point roughly north-east, at 48.1 degrees: about the direction of the midsummer sunrise. “I got really, really excited about that,” said Harding.

Wessex Archaeology recruited Fabio Silva, a skyscape archaeologist at consultancy Stone x Sky, to study the alignment of the postholes more carefully. Using a 3D reconstruction of the landscape with modern buildings removed, plus data on the sun’s shifting path across the sky, Silva determined that the postholes were neatly aligned with the past summer solstice sunrise.

Strictly, the alignment was about 1 degree out, but Silva said this makes sense once you realise that the wooden posts could have been up to 50 centimetres across. “You have to take that width into account,” he said at the press conference, in which case the alignment is “bang on”. “The odds of this being by chance are less than 0.5 per cent,” he said.

“Probably a rough orientation is good enough for the ritual that you are supposed to carry out in these sites,” says A. César González-García at the Spanish National Research Council in Santiago de Compostela, who wasn’t involved in the study. “It looks like there is a broad understanding and interest in the sky.”

Older sites in the area also show evidence of people tracking the sun, albeit with less precision. “From the earliest times that we have Neolithic people present in that landscape, they are incorporating the sun into their ceremonial architecture,” said Matt Leivers, also at Wessex Archaeology.

“We’ve got loads of timber monuments that have those kind of alignments,” says Greaney. The Bulford monument “is adding another one to that, potentially, but much earlier”.

For instance, at nearby Larkhill, there is a Neolithic enclosure from around 3700 BC, well before Bulford and Stonehenge. Its entrance faces roughly north-east. If you stand in that entrance and look at Sidbury Hill (the highest point on the horizon) on midsummer morning, “you see the sun rise dead ahead of you over Sidbury Hill”, said Leivers.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a 5000-year-old wooden monument aligned to the summer solstice, predating Stonehenge, represents a significant archaeological breakthrough that deepens understanding of ancient human knowledge and cultural practices. This finding has broad historical and cultural significance for the general public, providing tangible evidence of early astronomical awareness and ritual behavior in Neolithic Britain. The article offers detailed context about the discovery, its dating, and its implications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Ancient wooden monument predates Stonehenge by 500 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Archaeologists have long studied Stonehenge, a famous stone circle in the UK built around 3100 BC. Stonehenge is known for its alignment with the summer solstice, the longest day of the year, when the sun rises in a special spot on the horizon.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered remains of a wooden monument near the village of Bulford, about 5 kilometres from Stonehenge. This wooden structure dates back 5000 years, or around 2950 BC, making it 500 years older than Stonehenge’s stones. The monument included two large postholes that lined up with the sunrise on the summer solstice.

**Impact** – This find shows that people in Britain were tracking the sun’s movement and building monuments to mark important solar events even before Stonehenge was built. It is one of the earliest examples of such astronomical knowledge in the British Isles. The discovery helps us understand how ancient people observed nature and practiced rituals connected to the sun.

**What’s next step** – Archaeologists will continue studying the Bulford site and other nearby locations to learn more about how early communities used these monuments. They may also explore how these wooden structures influenced later stone monuments like Stonehenge.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A wooden monument built 500 years before Stonehenge shows ancient Britons were observing the summer solstice much earlier than previously known.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un ancien monument en bois précède Stonehenge de 500 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les archéologues étudient depuis longtemps Stonehenge, un cercle de pierres célèbre au Royaume-Uni construit vers 3100 avant J.-C. Stonehenge est connu pour son alignement avec le solstice d’été, le jour le plus long de l’année, lorsque le soleil se lève à un endroit spécial à l’horizon.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert les vestiges d’un monument en bois près du village de Bulford, à environ 5 kilomètres de Stonehenge. Cette structure en bois date d’il y a 5000 ans, vers 2950 avant J.-C., soit 500 ans avant les pierres de Stonehenge. Le monument comprenait deux grands trous de poteaux alignés avec le lever du soleil au solstice d’été.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les habitants de la Grande-Bretagne observaient le mouvement du soleil et construisaient des monuments pour marquer des événements solaires importants bien avant Stonehenge. C’est l’un des premiers exemples de connaissances astronomiques dans les îles britanniques. Cette découverte aide à comprendre comment les anciens observaient la nature et pratiquaient des rituels liés au soleil.

**Prochaine étape** – Les archéologues vont continuer à étudier le site de Bulford et d’autres endroits proches pour en apprendre davantage sur l’utilisation de ces monuments par les premières communautés. Ils pourront aussi explorer comment ces structures en bois ont influencé les monuments en pierre comme Stonehenge.

**Phrase clé** – Un monument en bois construit 500 ans avant Stonehenge montre que les anciens Britanniques observaient le solstice d’été bien plus tôt qu’on ne le pensait.
articles.personality_title_es: Un antiguo monumento de madera es 500 años anterior a Stonehenge
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los arqueólogos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo Stonehenge, un famoso círculo de piedras en el Reino Unido construido alrededor del año 3100 a.C. Stonehenge es conocido por alinearse con el solsticio de verano, el día más largo del año, cuando el sol sale en un lugar especial en el horizonte.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron restos de un monumento de madera cerca del pueblo de Bulford, a unos 5 kilómetros de Stonehenge. Esta estructura de madera tiene unos 5000 años, o sea alrededor del 2950 a.C., lo que la hace 500 años más antigua que las piedras de Stonehenge. El monumento incluía dos grandes agujeros para postes que se alineaban con la salida del sol en el solsticio de verano.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que las personas en Gran Bretaña observaban el movimiento del sol y construían monumentos para marcar eventos solares importantes mucho antes de Stonehenge. Es uno de los primeros ejemplos de conocimiento astronómico en las Islas Británicas. El descubrimiento ayuda a entender cómo los antiguos observaban la naturaleza y realizaban rituales relacionados con el sol.

**Próximo paso** – Los arqueólogos seguirán estudiando el sitio de Bulford y otros lugares cercanos para aprender más sobre cómo las primeras comunidades usaban estos monumentos. También podrían investigar cómo estas estructuras de madera influyeron en los monumentos de piedra como Stonehenge.

**Frase para recordar** – Un monumento de madera construido 500 años antes que Stonehenge muestra que los antiguos británicos observaban el solsticio de verano mucho antes de lo que se creía.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two tall wooden posts standing 120 meters apart on a gently rolling Salisbury Plain landscape at dawn, aligned precisely with the glowing midsummer sunrise on the horizon, surrounded by subtle outlines of ancient Neolithic pottery shards embedded in the earth and faint chalk rubble, under a soft sky of natural earthy tones.

articles.title: Press release - A solid start - first progress report on the “One Europe, One Market" roadmap
articles.summary: In the first review of the Joint Declaration and Roadmap, the EU institutions took stock of progress made and reaffirmed their commitment to timely delivery. Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
Following the signature of the Joint Declaration on the EU legislative priorities for 2026 and of the ‘One Europe, One Market’ Roadmap, the European Parliament, the Council and the Commission have worked closely together to effectively deliver on all priorities identified therein. Progress will be reviewed on a quarterly basis. The June 2026 Progress Review marks the first monitoring exercise on the implementation of both documents. The institutions reaffirm their commitment to this process and to the timely and effective delivery and implementation of the Joint Declaration and Roadmap.

«Europe has the talent, the capital and the ideas. Our task now is to remove the barriers that prevent companies from turning that potential into growth, innovation and investment. The One Europe, One Market Roadmap is our determination to swiftly deliver on all the key initiatives that will drive Europe’s competitiveness. The European Parliament will keep up the pace, while ensuring that the quality of our legislation matches the ambition of our goals, so that companies can innovate, scale up and thrive in Europe. »

Roberta Metsola, President of the European Parliament

«When we signed the ‘One Europe, One Market’ roadmap in Cyprus, we agreed on more than a plan - we agreed on concrete action. This has been the guiding principle of the Cyprus Presidency over the past six months, as we have worked relentlessly to strengthen the EU's competitiveness, resilience, and prosperity, with our citizens and businesses at the heart of every proposal.

Today, we are proving that our joint commitment was not just words. We are delivering on our priorities, tracking progress closely, and ensuring that the roadmap we set together continues to drive real results for Europe.»

Nikos Christodoulides, President of the Republic of Cyprus

“We have made a commitment: to complete the Single Market by the end of next year. This is our One Europe, one Market roadmap. Today’s progress report shows that we are well on our way. We have discussed how we can deliver on this promise to our citizens and business even faster. Together, we will unlock the full power of our 450 million people-strong Single Market."

Ursula von der Leyen, President of the European Commission
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the tangible progress and strong institutional commitment toward completing the EU Single Market, which promises broad economic benefits and increased competitiveness for 450 million Europeans. The focus on removing barriers to growth, innovation, and investment has significant real-world impact and societal relevance across the EU. The article provides clear context about the roadmap, progress review, and leadership statements, showing substantive advancement on a major policy initiative.
articles.personality_title: EU institutions report solid progress on ‘One Europe, One Market’ plan
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 2026, the European Parliament, the Council, and the European Commission agreed on a plan called the ‘One Europe, One Market’ roadmap. This plan aims to improve the EU Single Market by removing barriers that stop companies from growing, innovating, and investing across Europe.

**What happened** – In June 2026, these EU institutions completed their first progress review since signing the plan. They checked how much work has been done and confirmed they are committed to finishing the roadmap on time. Leaders from each institution shared positive updates about the progress and their determination to keep moving forward.

**Impact** – This progress is important because the EU Single Market includes 450 million people. Making it easier for companies to work across borders can help create more jobs, new ideas, and better products. The review shows real steps are being taken to boost Europe’s economy and help businesses thrive.

**What's next step** – The EU will keep reviewing progress every three months to make sure the plan stays on track. The goal is to complete the Single Market improvements by the end of 2027, unlocking more opportunities for citizens and businesses.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU is making steady progress toward a stronger, more connected Single Market that will help millions of people and businesses grow across Europe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les institutions de l’UE annoncent des progrès solides sur le plan « Une Europe, un marché »
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 2026, le Parlement européen, le Conseil et la Commission européenne ont adopté un plan appelé la feuille de route « Une Europe, un marché ». Ce plan vise à améliorer le marché unique de l’UE en supprimant les obstacles qui empêchent les entreprises de se développer, d’innover et d’investir dans toute l’Europe.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En juin 2026, ces institutions ont réalisé leur premier bilan depuis la signature du plan. Elles ont vérifié les avancées réalisées et confirmé leur engagement à respecter les délais. Les dirigeants ont partagé des bilans positifs et leur détermination à poursuivre les efforts.

**Impact** – Ces progrès sont importants car le marché unique de l’UE concerne 450 millions de personnes. Faciliter le travail des entreprises à travers les frontières peut créer plus d’emplois, d’idées nouvelles et de meilleurs produits. Le bilan montre que des mesures concrètes sont prises pour renforcer l’économie européenne.

**Prochaine étape** – L’UE continuera à faire un point tous les trois mois pour s’assurer que le plan avance bien. L’objectif est de finaliser les améliorations du marché unique d’ici fin 2027, ouvrant ainsi plus d’opportunités pour les citoyens et les entreprises.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – L’UE progresse régulièrement vers un marché unique plus fort et plus connecté, qui aidera des millions de personnes et d’entreprises à se développer en Europe.
articles.personality_title_es: Las instituciones de la UE informan de un sólido avance en el plan ‘Una Europa, un mercado’
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 2026, el Parlamento Europeo, el Consejo y la Comisión Europea acordaron un plan llamado la hoja de ruta ‘Una Europa, un mercado’. Este plan busca mejorar el Mercado Único de la UE eliminando obstáculos que impiden que las empresas crezcan, innoven e inviertan en toda Europa.

**Qué pasó** – En junio de 2026, estas instituciones realizaron su primera revisión del progreso desde que firmaron el plan. Revisaron cuánto se ha avanzado y confirmaron su compromiso de cumplir con los plazos. Los líderes compartieron actualizaciones positivas y su determinación para seguir adelante.

**Impacto** – Este avance es importante porque el Mercado Único de la UE incluye a 450 millones de personas. Facilitar que las empresas trabajen entre países puede crear más empleos, nuevas ideas y mejores productos. La revisión muestra que se están tomando medidas reales para fortalecer la economía europea.

**Próximo paso** – La UE seguirá revisando el progreso cada tres meses para asegurar que el plan sigue en camino. El objetivo es completar las mejoras del Mercado Único para finales de 2027, abriendo más oportunidades para ciudadanos y empresas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La UE avanza de manera constante hacia un Mercado Único más fuerte y conectado que ayudará a millones de personas y empresas a crecer en toda Europa.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of three interconnected golden gears engraved with stylized European stars, smoothly turning together above a vibrant map outline of Europe formed by interlocking puzzle pieces, all bathed in warm, natural earth tones symbolizing unity, cooperation, and steady progress toward a shared market.
